findings.description: Inverted expiry check: `if session.expires_at > now` deletes/rejects sessions that are still LIVE and the fall-through `return session.user_id` only fires when `expires_at <= now`, so EXPIRED sessions are accepted as valid while valid sessions are rejected (the comparison must be `<=` to expire, opposite of cleanup_expired on line 148 which is correct).

SQL injection via f-string interpolation of `email` into the login query (e.g. email `' OR '1'='1' --` authenticates as an arbitrary active user); also a logic fault since any email containing a single quote raises sqlite3.OperationalError and crashes login — fix by parameterizing with `WHERE email = ? AND is_active = 1`.

Lockout uses `failed_attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS` and the counter is incremented to MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS on the 5th wrong password, but that 5th failing attempt still runs the password check and increments before returning, and on the 6th attempt the lock triggers; combined with the fact that LOCKOUT_MINUTES is never read anywhere, the lockout is permanent (no time-based reset) rather than the intended 15-minute window.

LOCKOUT_MINUTES constant is declared but never referenced; failed_attempts is only ever reset to 0 on a successful login (line 82), so once an account hits MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS it is locked forever with no expiry, contradicting the time-bounded lockout the constant implies.

Sessions are stored only in the in-memory `self._sessions` dict, so all active sessions are silently lost on process restart and are not shared across multiple worker processes/instances, causing users to be logged out unpredictably and validate_session to fail for tokens issued by another worker.

`datetime.utcnow()` returns a naive datetime; if any session timestamps ever cross a tz-aware boundary (serialization, comparison with aware datetimes elsewhere) comparisons raise `TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes`, and utcnow() is deprecated as of Python 3.12 — prefer `datetime.now(timezone.utc)`.

The failed-attempt increment commits but there is no upper-bound guard or row-existence recheck; if two concurrent failed logins for the same user interleave across separate connections the increment is a read-modify-write at the SQL level (atomic per statement) but the surrounding lock decision on line 70 was read from a stale snapshot, so the effective threshold can be exceeded by concurrent requests (lockout race).

find_user_by_email runs `SELECT * FROM users`, materializes every row into User objects, then linear-scans in Python for a matching email instead of using a `WHERE email = ?` clause — O(n) over the full table per lookup and unbounded memory growth as the user table scales.

_create_session unconditionally overwrites self._sessions[token] with no collision check; token_urlsafe(32) makes collisions astronomically unlikely, but rotate_token logs the old session out (line 142) then creates a new one with no rollback, so if _create_session were to raise the user is left with no session and the validation already passed — minor state-consistency gap.

Timing side channel: when email is unknown (row is None, line 59) login returns early without performing any pbkdf2 hashing, whereas a known email performs the expensive 100k-iteration hash, letting an attacker distinguish valid from invalid emails by response time (user enumeration) — note primarily for critic-security.

validate_session never lazily evicts genuinely expired sessions on the correct branch (because the expiry logic is inverted on line 105); even after fixing line 105, the function returns user_id without refreshing/sliding expiry, so cleanup_expired (line 145) is the only reclamation path and must be scheduled externally or the dict grows with abandoned-but-unexpired tokens.
